rxn_class_summary: Diastereoselective electrocatalytic 1,2-diamination of di- and tri-substituted alkenes with sulfamides using a triarylamine redox mediator, producing cyclic sulfamides with H2 evolution at the cathode
electrochemical_role: Anode oxidizes tris(2,4-dibromophenyl)amine mediator to its aminium radical cation, which performs outer-sphere SET oxidation of the alkene to a radical cation; cathode reduces protons to H2

key_intermediates.name: β-amino carbon radical (IV) from sulfamide addition to alkene radical cation
β-amino carbocation (V) after second SET oxidation

electron_flow: Anode oxidizes triarylamine 1 to NAr3•+, which performs SET oxidation of the alkene to a radical cation; the sulfamide N–H adds to give a β-amino C-radical, which is oxidized by a second equivalent of NAr3•+ to a carbocation that cyclizes diastereoselectively to the 5-membered cyclic sulfamide. Protons released are reduced at the Pt cathode to H2.

probe_reagents.name: triarylamine redox catalysts of varying Ep/2 (1, 6, 7, 8)
probe_reagents.observation: Higher-potential mediator 1 (Ep/2 = 1.48 V vs SCE) gave product; lower-potential analogs 6–8 (1.06–1.33 V) were less effective, consistent with need to oxidize alkene (Ep/2 = 1.66–1.80 V)

probe_reagents.name: pure E- vs Z-isomer of alkene 2
probe_reagents.observation: Both isomers converged to the same trans diastereomer 5 (>20:1 dr; 65% and 69% yield) supporting a stepwise radical-cation pathway with stereochemistry set during cyclization

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of 1, 2, 3 (and mixtures)
probe_reagents.observation: Catalytic current enhancement upon mixing 1 with alkenes 2 and 3 confirms mediator-based electrocatalytic oxidation of the alkenes

notes: Stereoselectivity is rationalized by cyclization of cationic intermediate V placing R1/R2 trans on the nascent 5-membered ring; no radical traps (TEMPO/BHT), KIE, or DFT are explicitly shown on these pages. Alternative direct anodic oxidation of alkene is disfavored because of self-dimerization and electrode-surface reactivity, motivating the indirect (mediated) electrolysis.
